What's going on ladies and gents? We are at advanced funnel strategy. So if you know how to get your ads working and humming, the next step is naturally to push them to a funnel that is optimized for conversions. And there's a lot more, there's like transformers, there's a lot more than to funnel building that meets the eye, funnelology. So you'll have a PhD in funnelology by the end of this. All right, so what I wanna go over since just about everybody's watching this is in service business, the three best funnels for service. Now there's a million different types of funnels and strategies that are out there, but these are the three best in my experience for service. And that includes, you know, super, super, super high ticket like our stuff, mid-ticket, low-ticket, all of that kind of stuff in between. And so the three that we're gonna cover are opt-ins, which is the best for volume for low to medium ticket. Now, if I'm going into a new service category, a new offer, the first type of funnel that I'm gonna try out is gonna be opt-in funnels. And that's because the first thing I'm gonna do is get lead flow. And then from there, you can increase lead quality. All right, so sequentially, the first thing that would happen is opt-ins, right? The next thing would be an application funnel. So if you're like, okay, I get it, this offer is working, the ads are working, I'm getting too many people to service or they're not the lead quality I want, blah, blah, blah. Right? And so that's when you would introduce an application style funnel, which is better for high ticket. And then the third option is, once you have gotten your application funnel going to keep it evergreen, then you're going to want to put different widgets on the front end. And a widget is just kind of a placeholder for what I'm saying is just something that, some people say lead magnets, I just don't really like the term that much because it's just a widget, you know what I mean? It's something that someone's gonna use. And then once you have flow, then optimize the flow. All right, so quick overview for definitions visually. A typical opt-in funnel would be a very simple funnel with a local offer and it doesn't have to be local if you're not local, but like whatever your offer is, subheading that's gonna be a big benefit to why. And then you can optionally add scarcity in and then a button. Now underneath of that button, there's two styles of opt-in pages that I've seen that worked best. One is the simple, simple opt-in, just like this, which has nothing else. So then you have very few variables to opt in. Little square here is optional, but it's where if you wanna throw in an application at the end so that you can have even more qualified leads and really those are gonna be the hottest, hottest leads, allow them to apply, which would be kind of a presale questionnaire and the people who fill that out are gonna be by far the most qualified. If you don't feel like doing that, you can always just throw them straight to a Facebook group, either of those options work. But this is typically the best strategy for overall volume, and especially when you're going into a new market, okay? Now, the next style is an application style, right? And so this is what we want. So you have schedule, then presale questionnaire, rather than the tried and true presale questionnaire, then schedule. The reason that I think the V1 is working a little bit better is that you get a little bit of a micro commitment from people to pick their time. Once they pick their time, then it's like, okay, I guess I should fill out this presale questionnaire. And that's where you want the heading for the presale questionnaire to be like, hey, you know, your appointment time, you have to fill this out before your appointment time or we will cancel it. And so then it's like, they've made the micro commitment, they don't want to have wasted the time that they just put into it. And so they fill out the presale questionnaire. You can get away with longer questionnaires on the thank you page, the thank you thank you page, the third page, then you can on the second page. And I think that's why V1 is probably the strategy I would use to start out with. V2, that being said, I spent more money on, but V1 seems to be working a little bit better. Both of it, like I've spent more money on it so that the traffic will convert on the second page there, so that you can get people to actually buy the main thing that you're trying to offer. So I use, I've got 11 useful frameworks to make gyms wildly profitable, that's a thing. But what I want them to schedule is a call. And so the same thing would be like, I want to give you a cookbook on, or like a five video series, whatever it is. And the second page is the application, sorry, is the scheduling page. And then the third page is a redirect to a Facebook group. So that is kind of how our funnel overall works. And it works really well. So that, I just want to be clear here that this second row is the one that you're going to be spending the majority of your time optimizing, because that's the one that's gonna make you the money. All right, so I just want to give you a high level overview of what the three primary funnel types look like for service before we dive in to the advanced funnel math.
